content.text: Data readiness is often the primary reason GenAI use cases fail to reach production — not technical feasibility or cost. A well-selected integration pattern running against poor-quality, inaccessible, or non-consented data will produce worse outcomes than a well-engineered rule-based system. The data readiness assessment evaluates five dimensions across the data that the use case will depend on: data for grounding (RAG), data for evaluation, and data that flows through the model at inference time. Each dimension receives a 1–5 score; the weighted composite determines whether the use case is ready to proceed to build.

attrs.alt: Five data readiness dimensions: Availability, Quality, Relevance, Labelling, Governance

content.text: The Five Data Readiness Dimensions

Each of the five dimensions captures a different failure mode. A use case that scores well on availability but poorly on governance is still a hard blocker. A use case that scores well on quality but poorly on relevance will produce confidently wrong outputs. All five dimensions must be assessed.
Dimension 1: Availability
Does the data exist, and can the build team access it within the delivery timeline?
What to assess

— Identify the source systems for every data asset the use case requires. For RAG patterns, this means the corpus of documents to be indexed. For grading/evaluation, this means labelled historical examples. For inference-time data, this means the data the model will receive as input during operation.
Availability blockers

— Data exists in a system that requires a 6-month procurement or access approval process; data is held by a third party that requires a data sharing agreement; data exists but is archived in formats that require significant processing (e.g. COBOL binary files, legacy database dumps without schema documentation); volume is insufficient for meaningful evaluation (fewer than 200 examples for a classification task).
Score 5
— Data is in an accessible system with a working API or documented export process; access rights are already granted or grantable within 2 weeks; volume is sufficient.
Score 1
— Data is not accessible within the delivery timeline due to procurement, legal, or technical barriers.
Dimension 2: Quality
Is the data accurate, complete, and consistent enough for the model to learn from or be grounded by?
Completeness
— What fraction of records have non-null values in the fields the model will use? A >15% null rate in a key field is a red flag requiring either data cleaning or a fallback strategy.
Accuracy

— For grounding data (RAG), inaccurate source documents produce inaccurate model outputs regardless of prompt quality. If the organisation's policy documents contain errors or outdated information that has not been corrected in the document management system, those errors surface directly in model responses.
Consistency

— The same entity (a customer, a claim, a product) described differently in different source systems causes the model to give inconsistent answers depending on which system's data is retrieved. Check whether a master data management (MDM) layer exists or whether the build team will need to reconcile inconsistencies in the ingestion pipeline.
Freshness

— For dynamic domains (pricing, regulatory requirements, product catalogue), assess how quickly the source data is updated and whether the ingestion pipeline can keep the model's grounding data current. A RAG index that is 6 months stale in a dynamic domain is worse than no RAG.
Dimension 3: Relevance
Does the data contain the right signal for this specific use case, or is it from a sufficiently different domain, geography, or product line that the model will be grounded in irrelevant information?
Domain match
— Policy wordings for commercial liability insurance are not a useful grounding corpus for a personal motor claims assistant. Even within the same insurer, product-line boundaries matter.
Distribution

— If the historical examples used for evaluation are all from one month, one region, or one customer segment, evaluation accuracy may not reflect production performance across the full input distribution.
Edge case coverage

— For use cases where failure on edge cases is high-risk (fraud detection, coverage denial), assess whether the data includes sufficient edge case examples. If 99% of historical examples are straightforward cases, the model will appear highly accurate in evaluation while performing poorly on the 1% of cases that matter most.
Dimension 4: Labelling
Is ground truth available to evaluate the model's outputs? Without an evaluation set, accuracy cannot be measured — and without measurable accuracy, production readiness cannot be demonstrated.
For classification tasks

— A minimum of 200 labelled examples per class is generally required for statistically meaningful evaluation, with at least 50 held out for test (not used during prompt development). Fewer than 50 per class makes it difficult to detect performance differences between prompt versions.
For generation tasks

— Labelling is harder: expert reviewers must rate generated outputs on relevant dimensions (accuracy, completeness, tone). Inter-annotator agreement (IAA) — how consistently two reviewers assign the same rating — must be measured and meet a minimum threshold (Cohen's Kappa > 0.6 is a common bar). An IAA score below 0.4 indicates that the evaluation schema is ambiguous and cannot produce reliable quality measurements.
For RAG tasks

— Create a set of representative questions with expert-verified reference answers. The evaluation measures whether the model's retrieved context contains the correct answer and whether the model correctly derives the answer from it. These are independent failure modes: retrieval failure (wrong document retrieved) and generation failure (correct document retrieved but wrong answer generated).
Labelling anti-pattern

— Proxy labels from operational metrics (e.g. 'the adjuster approved the recommendation' as a label for 'the recommendation was correct') are often used when expert labelling is expensive. Proxy labels conflate model quality with other factors (adjuster workload, policy changes, process shortcuts) and systematically understate the model's failure rate.
Dimension 5: Governance
Are the legal, privacy, and ethical constraints satisfied for using this data with a GenAI model? Governance failures are hard blockers — no amount of data quality compensates for a legal barrier.
Consent for AI use

— Was personal data collected with consent that covers AI model use? Consumer data collected under consent terms that specify 'customer service' or 'fraud prevention' may not cover use for model training, fine-tuning, or analysis. Legal review is required before using consumer data in any GenAI context.
Data residency

— GDPR requires that EU personal data remain within the EU (or countries with adequacy decisions). UK financial regulators require that data subject to FCA rules remain within the UK. Sending personal data to a US-based model API endpoint may violate these requirements. Check whether the chosen model provider offers a data processing agreement (DPA) and an EU/UK endpoint.
Sending data to third-party models

— Sending proprietary or confidential data (trade secrets, unreleased financial results, medical records, legal advice) to a third-party model API requires that the provider's contract includes a data processing agreement that prohibits training on that data. Most major providers offer opt-out-of-training terms in enterprise agreements; confirm this is in place before build begins.
Bias and fairness

— For use cases where the model's output affects individuals (credit decisions, claims decisions, hiring recommendations), assess whether the training data or grounding corpus contains historical biases that the model could perpetuate. This is particularly relevant when using historical decisions as labels: if past decisions were biased, the model learns to replicate that bias.
Data Readiness Scoring Model

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal, Optional
import math

DataReadinessDimension = Literal[
    "availability", "quality", "relevance", "labelling", "governance"
]

WEIGHTS = {
    "availability": 0.25,
    "quality":      0.25,
    "relevance":    0.20,
    "labelling":    0.15,
    "governance":   0.15,
}

@dataclass
class DataReadinessAssessment:
    use_case: str
    pattern: Literal["RAG", "AgentLoop", "GatewayOnly", "FineTuning"]
    # Score each dimension 1–5
    availability: int   # 1 = data inaccessible, 5 = pipeline ready
    quality:      int   # 1 = poor / unknown, 5 = high quality, profiled
    relevance:    int   # 1 = off-domain, 5 = precisely on-domain
    labelling:    int   # 1 = no labels, 5 = expert labels + IAA score known
    governance:   int   # 1 = hard legal blocker, 5 = fully cleared
    # Optional: known gaps
    gaps: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def weighted_score(self) -> float:
        raw = (
            self.availability * WEIGHTS["availability"] +
            self.quality      * WEIGHTS["quality"]      +
            self.relevance    * WEIGHTS["relevance"]    +
            self.labelling    * WEIGHTS["labelling"]    +
            self.governance   * WEIGHTS["governance"]
        )
        return round(raw, 2)

    @property
    def has_hard_blocker(self) -> bool:
        # Governance score 1 = legal blocker — always a hard stop.
        # For RAG, availability 1 = cannot build index — hard stop.
        if self.governance <= 1:
            return True
        if self.pattern in ("RAG", "AgentLoop") and self.availability <= 1:
            return True
        return False

    def readiness_band(self) -> str:
        s = self.weighted_score
        if s >= 4.0: return "GREEN — proceed to build"
        if s >= 3.0: return "AMBER — proceed with documented mitigations"
        if s >= 2.0: return "RED — address gaps before build"
        return "BLOCKED — resolve hard blockers first"

    def report(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"=== Data Readiness: {self.use_case} ({self.pattern}) ===",
            f"  Availability : {self.availability}/5",
            f"  Quality      : {self.quality}/5",
            f"  Relevance    : {self.relevance}/5",
            f"  Labelling    : {self.labelling}/5",
            f"  Governance   : {self.governance}/5",
            f"  Weighted score: {self.weighted_score}/5.00",
            f"  Status: {self.readiness_band()}",
        ]
        if self.has_hard_blocker:
            lines.append("  *** HARD BLOCKER — do not proceed to build ***")
        if self.gaps:
            lines.append("  Gaps:")
            for g in self.gaps:
                lines.append(f"    - {g}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Policy coverage assistant (RAG pattern)
policy_rag = DataReadinessAssessment(
    use_case="Coverage Verification Assistant",
    pattern="RAG",
    availability=3,   # PDFs exist; OCR pipeline not yet built
    quality=4,        # Policies are authoritative, but OCR introduces noise
    relevance=5,      # Policy corpus is exactly the target domain
    labelling=2,      # No labelled Q&A pairs for evaluation yet
    governance=5,     # Internal documents; no personal data; legally cleared
    gaps=[
        "OCR pre-processing pipeline must be built before ingestion (3 weeks)",
        "Evaluation set (200 labelled Q&A pairs) must be created by subject-matter experts",
    ],
)
print(policy_rag.report())

print()

# FNOL classification (Gateway pattern)
fnol = DataReadinessAssessment(
    use_case="FNOL Auto-Classification",
    pattern="GatewayOnly",
    availability=5,   # Free-text from web form; available at API layer
    quality=4,        # Claimant text is noisy; occasional gibberish entries
    relevance=5,      # Exactly the data the model will process in production
    labelling=4,      # Historical FNOL records have adjuster-assigned categories
    governance=3,     # Contains PII; must not be logged to model provider
    gaps=[
        "PII redaction at gateway layer required before logging (2 weeks)",
        "Adjuster-category label mapping to FNOL taxonomy needs reconciliation (1 week)",
    ],
)
print(fnol.report())
Grounding Feasibility for RAG Patterns

For use cases that will use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), the data readiness assessment must include a grounding feasibility check that goes beyond the five dimensions. The RAG pattern's effectiveness depends on the quality of the chunking strategy, the embedding model's ability to represent the domain semantics, and the vector index's retrieval precision.
Chunking Strategy Assessment

The chunking strategy determines how source documents are split into segments before embedding. Poor chunking — too large, too small, or cutting across semantic boundaries — degrades retrieval quality regardless of document quality.

def chunk_policy_document(text: str, max_tokens: int = 400, overlap: int = 50) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Split a policy document into overlapping chunks suitable for embedding.
    Uses paragraph boundaries to avoid cutting mid-sentence.
    """
    paragraphs = [p.strip() for p in text.split("\n\n") if p.strip()]
    chunks = []
    current_tokens = 0
    current_paras = []

    def approx_tokens(s: str) -> int:
        # ~1.3 tokens per word for English legal text
        return int(len(s.split()) * 1.3)

    for para in paragraphs:
        pt = approx_tokens(para)
        if current_tokens + pt > max_tokens and current_paras:
            chunk_text = " ".join(current_paras)
            chunks.append({
                "text": chunk_text,
                "token_count": approx_tokens(chunk_text),
            })
            # Keep last `overlap`-token worth of paragraphs for continuity
            while current_paras and current_tokens > overlap:
                removed = current_paras.pop(0)
                current_tokens -= approx_tokens(removed)
        current_paras.append(para)
        current_tokens += pt

    if current_paras:
        chunk_text = " ".join(current_paras)
        chunks.append({
            "text": chunk_text,
            "token_count": approx_tokens(chunk_text),
        })

    return chunks

# Example: 3-sentence policy clause
policy_text = """
Coverage under Section 3 applies to sudden and accidental water damage arising from
the failure of a permanently installed plumbing system within the insured premises.

This coverage does not apply to gradual leakage, seepage, or damage caused by
failure to maintain the plumbing system in reasonable working order.

The maximum limit of indemnity for any one occurrence under this section is the
lesser of the cost of repair and the sum insured stated in the Schedule.
"""

chunks = chunk_policy_document(policy_text, max_tokens=150)
for i, c in enumerate(chunks):
    print(f"Chunk {i+1} ({c['token_count']} tokens): {c['text'][:80]}...")
Too large chunks

— A chunk that covers multiple policy sections may include the answer to the query but also a large amount of irrelevant text. The model's attention is diluted across the full chunk, reducing answer accuracy.
Too small chunks

— A chunk that covers a single sentence may not contain sufficient context for the model to answer correctly. 'This coverage does not apply to gradual leakage' requires surrounding context to understand what 'this coverage' refers to.
Boundary cuts
— Chunking at fixed character counts often cuts across sentence or paragraph boundaries. Use paragraph-aware chunking with overlap (as shown above) to preserve semantic continuity.
Typical chunk sizes by domain
— Legal/policy documents: 300–500 tokens with 50-token overlap; Knowledge base articles: 200–400 tokens; Technical documentation: 400–600 tokens; Email/message bodies: 100–200 tokens.
Embedding Model Domain Fit

General-purpose embedding models (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere embed-english-v3) perform well for standard English text but may underperform on highly domain-specific language (medical terminology, legal jargon, actuarial notation). Assess domain fit by measuring retrieval recall on a small sample before committing to a model:
Create 20–30 representative queries from the target domain (e.g. 'Does flooding caused by a burst pipe qualify as an escape of water loss?').
For each query, identify the correct source document(s) manually.
Embed the query and retrieve the top-5 results from the vector index.
Measure Recall@5: what fraction of the correct documents appear in the top 5.
If Recall@5 < 0.7, evaluate a domain-specific embedding model or fine-tune the general-purpose model on domain examples.
Data Readiness Scorecard

For use cases where data is sourced from multiple systems (common in RAG and Agent Loop patterns), produce a per-source-system scorecard rather than a single aggregate score. Each source is assessed independently; the weakest source sets the overall readiness ceiling:
Data Source
Availability
Quality
Relevance
Labelling
Governance
Composite
Status
Policy document corpus (PDF)
AMBER
Historical claims (CMS)
GREEN
Legal knowledge base
RED
FNOL web form text
GREEN (PII gap)

In this example, the legal knowledge base (availability 2, labelling 1) would block any use case that depends on it. A phased approach — delivering the use cases that depend on the AMBER/GREEN sources first while the RED source is resolved — is the recommended mitigation.
Common Data Readiness Anti-Patterns
Assuming historical data quality equals production data quality

— Data that was good enough for SQL reporting is not necessarily good enough for model grounding. Reporting queries tolerate null values with WHERE clauses; model prompts that include null-contaminated text produce confused outputs. Profile the data as the model will actually receive it, not as reporting uses it.
Conflating data volume with data readiness

— A data lake containing 10 years of claims records sounds ready. If those records are in 7 different schemas (due to system migrations), span 3 geographies with different policy structures, contain 40% null rates in free-text fields, and require legal review before use, volume is irrelevant. Quality, consistency, and governance are independent of volume.
Skipping the labelling assessment for generation tasks

— Teams frequently assess data readiness for the training/grounding data but skip the labelling assessment because 'we can evaluate outputs manually'. Manual evaluation is not repeatable, not scalable, and cannot detect gradual quality regressions after deployment. Invest in an evaluation set before build, not after.
Sending PII to the model without a DPA in place

— The most common governance failure. A developer prototyping with real customer data using a personal API key (no enterprise DPA, no opt-out of training) has already violated data processing obligations. Establish the enterprise DPA and PII redaction pipeline before the first non-synthetic data is sent to any model API.
Summary

Data readiness assessment covers five dimensions: Availability (can we access the data?), Quality (is it accurate and complete?), Relevance (is it the right data for this task?), Labelling (do we have ground truth for evaluation?), and Governance (are legal and privacy constraints met?).
Governance dimension score 1 and Availability dimension score 1 for RAG/Agent Loop patterns are hard blockers that prevent proceeding to build regardless of other scores.

For RAG patterns, grounding feasibility adds two more assessments: chunking strategy fit and embedding model domain recall. Recall@5 < 0.7 on a sample query set is a signal to evaluate domain-specific embedding models.
Multi-source use cases need a per-source scorecard. The weakest source sets the overall readiness ceiling. Phase delivery to lead with use cases that depend on ready data sources.

The most common anti-patterns are: assuming historical data quality equals production data quality; conflating volume with readiness; skipping labelling assessment for generation tasks; and sending PII to external model APIs without an enterprise data processing agreement.
